facts_disclosed: Music producer and DJ in Toronto
Uses Ableton Live as primary DAW

turns.content: yooo okay so I need help figuring out acoustic treatment for my studio!! the lab is sounding MAD boxy rn and I cant mix properly its driving me CRAZY

That boxy sound is really common in small studios! It's usually caused by standing waves and early reflections bouncing between parallel walls. A few questions to help figure out the best approach:

1. How big is your room roughly? (dimensions if you know them)
2. Are the walls drywall, concrete, or something else?
3. What kind of music are you producing — lots of bass-heavy stuff?
its like maybe 10x12 feet?? drywall walls. and YEA heavy bass — I produce dancehall, afrobeats, some hip hop. I DJ too so I need the low end to translate properly when I play out at venues still

Okay, 10x12 with drywall and bass-heavy genres — that explains the boxiness perfectly. Drywall resonates at low frequencies and a room that size will have nasty standing waves around 90-140Hz.

Here's what I'd prioritize:

1. **Bass traps in the corners** — this is the single biggest upgrade. Floor-to-ceiling bass traps in at least 2 corners (ideally all 4). Rockwool/mineral wool panels, minimum 4 inches thick. This will clean up the low end dramatically.

2. **First reflection points** — put 2-inch absorption panels on the side walls at ear level where sound bounces from your monitors to your listening position. Use the mirror trick: sit in your mix position, have someone slide a mirror along the wall. Where you see your speaker in the mirror, that's where the panel goes.

3. **Rear wall diffusion** — don't absorb everything back there, use a diffuser to scatter the sound.

For DJing and producing, getting that low end right is everything. Are you working with nearfield monitors or headphones mostly?
nearfields!! KRK Rokit 8s. they go LOW which is why I got them but honestly in this room the bass just builds up in the corners and I cant tell whats real and whats the room lying to me ahlie

Ahh yeah, the Rokit 8s push a LOT of low end already, and in a 10x12 untreated room they're going to excite every standing wave in there. The room is definitely lying to you — you're probably making mixing decisions based on what the room is adding, not what's actually in the track.

Honest suggestion: before you even buy treatment, download a free room measurement tool like REW (Room EQ Wizard). Play a frequency sweep through your KRKs and measure with a cheap measurement mic. It'll show you exactly which frequencies are building up and where the nulls are. That way you're not guessing — you'll know exactly what to treat.

For the corner bass traps, you can DIY them pretty cheap:
- Rockwool Safe'n'Sound batts (available at Home Depot in Toronto)
- Build simple wooden frames
- Cover with breathable fabric

You're looking at maybe $200-300 CAD for 4 corner traps if you build them yourself vs. $800+ buying pre-made. What DAW are you working in? Some have built-in room correction tools too.

ABLETON!! Ableton Live is my WHOLE LIFE fam. I do everything in there — beats, arrangement, mixing, the whole ting. Been on it since I was in school and the workflow just HITS different. nothing else comes close for how I work

Ableton is a beast for production workflow, no doubt! The Session View alone makes it incredible for building ideas and DJing. For your room correction situation though, Ableton doesn't have a built-in room correction tool like Logic's.

But here's what you can do:

1. **Sonarworks SoundID Reference** — it's a plugin that sits on your master bus and corrects for your room and your specific KRK monitors. It measures your room with a calibration mic and builds a correction profile. Around $100-150 CAD. It won't fix the room physically but it gives you a much more accurate picture while mixing.

2. **Use Ableton's EQ Eight** with a spectrum analyzer to visually check your low end against reference tracks. Load a pro track you know sounds good, A/B it with your mix, and see where the bass differs.

But real talk — the physical treatment (bass traps + panels) will do way more than any software fix. Software correction is a bandaid. Treating the room actually solves the problem.

Want me to break down a full DIY bass trap build list with Canadian pricing?

YES please!! I literally need this. the lab needs to be RIGHT because I got a couple placements coming up and the mixes HAVE to be clean. cant be sending reference mixes that sound like they were mixed in a bathroom lol

Ha! Nobody wants that bathroom reverb on a placement mix. Okay here's your shopping list — all available in Toronto:

**Corner Bass Traps x4 (floor to ceiling):**
- Rockwool Safe'n'Sound 3" batts — ~$65/bag (you'll need 3-4 bags) = ~$220
- 1x3" lumber for frames — ~$40
- Breathable fabric (burlap or landscaping fabric works) — ~$30
- Staple gun + staples — ~$25 if you don't have one

**First Reflection Panels x4:**
- Rockwool Safe'n'Sound 2" batts — ~$55/bag (1-2 bags) = ~$80
- 1x2" lumber for frames — ~$25
- Same fabric

**Total: roughly $400-450 CAD**

Build order:
1. Bass traps first — biggest impact
2. First reflection panels second
3. Measure with REW after each step so you can hear/see the difference

For the placements, even just getting the 4 corner bass traps in will make a massive difference in your low-end clarity. You could knock those out in a weekend.

Where in Toronto is your studio? If you're near a Home Depot or Rona, they'll have everything.
the lab is in my apartment in Scarborough lol so yea theres a Home Depot not far. $450 is not bad at ALL honestly. way less than I expected. gonna hit the hardware store this weekend fr fr

Scarborough Home Depot will have everything you need! One tip — call ahead and ask if they have the Rockwool Safe'n'Sound in stock because sometimes they only carry the fire-rated stuff (which also works but costs more).

A few extra tips for the apartment setup:

- **Don't glue anything to the walls** — use Command hooks or French cleats so you get your deposit back
- **Start with the corners behind your monitors** — those two corners are where the most bass buildup happens in a typical desk-against-the-wall setup
- **Leave a 2-inch air gap** between the bass traps and the wall — this actually increases low frequency absorption significantly
- **Wear a mask** when cutting Rockwool — that stuff is itchy and the dust is no joke

Once you get them up, load one of your recent mixes and A/B it with a reference track. You're going to hear details in the low end you never knew were there. Those placements are going to sound clean.

Good luck this weekend — come back if you need help with the build or if you want to talk about the REW measurements after!
BLESS!! this is exactly what I needed. the riddim of the lab is about to change fr. thank you so much!! gonna document the whole build too lol

Love that energy! Documenting the build is smart too — other producers always want to see studio treatment on a budget. Before and after clips of the same mix would be fire content.

Quick recap:
1. Bass traps in all 4 corners (priority: the 2 behind your monitors)
2. First reflection panels at ear level on side walls
3. Air gap behind the traps
4. Measure with REW before and after
5. Don't glue anything — apartment-friendly mounting

Your mixes are going to level up once that room is treated properly. Good luck with the placements — go get it!
